text: Six laparoscopic ports are visible in the abdominal wall. The surgeon dissects the omentum from the transverse colon, progressing from the hepatic flexure to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon divides the left gastric artery using a vascular stapler. The right gastric artery is dissected and clipped, followed by the division of the right gastroepiploic artery with a vascular stapler. The pylorus is identified, and the duodenum is divided using the stapler.

The surgeon applies a suture to the jejunum to mark the Roux limb, ensuring correct anastomosis. The jejunum is divided 40 centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz, and the mesentery is divided using a vascular stapler. A jejuno-jejunostomy is performed with two staplers.
The surgeon applies three marking sutures to the jejunum, securing it to the abdominal wall in preparation for a feeding jejunostomy.

The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus within the mediastinum and transects it using a stapler. Two traction sutures are applied to the esophageal sidewalls. The roux limb is brought up, and a stapled esophago-jejunostomy is performed by opening the jejunum. The completeness of the anastomosis is verified by checking the donuts, and the jejunal defect is closed with a stapler.

The surgeon closes the hiatus by approximating the left and right crura. A drain is inserted into the surgical site. The specimen is removed through an enlarged port, with the margins appearing clear.
